Cancer Res. 92, February 2001 106 ID as compared to the control value, and enhanced by additional 20% DS but not SI, which in fact caused a decrease at 0.04% SI alone (P<0.05). Serum TSH was increased by ID, and further enhanced by 20% DS but not SI (P<0.01). Histopathologically, diffuse irregularity of hypertropic follicular cells and decreased colloid were observed in all of the ID-treated rats (Fig. 1, b, c and d). The most promi- nent lesion was noted in the thyroid of 20% DS+ID group, in which diffuse follicular hypertrophy and/or hyperplasia and lack of colloid were evident (Fig. 1b). However, in the thyroid of the SI+ID group (Fig. 1d), changes were lim- ited to mild to moderate irregularity of thyroid follicles. The thyroids without the ID treatment did not demonstrate apparent histological changes (Fig. 1a). Table III summa- rizes data for cell proliferation. The PCNA-labeling indi- ces (%) were elevated by ID but not DS or SI alone. The value for the DS+ID group, but not either of the SI+ID groups, was higher than that for the ID alone group, albeit without statistical significance. DISCUSSION The fact of thyroid enlargement due to excessive soy- bean intake, especially in women and children, has been known for half a century. Soy protein diet has been shown to increase T4, free T4 and TSH levels in animals, whereas T3 is generally not affected.15-17) Experimentally, several investigators have also reported the induction of goiter in iodine-deficient rats maintained on a soybean diet.12, 18) In the present study, 20% DS increased the serum T4 (P<0.01) and TSH levels and synergistically stimulated thyroid growth in rats exposed to the ID diet. The mecha- nisms underlying the co-goitrogenic effect of excess DS and ID remain to be elucidated. SI, such as genistein and daidzein, which are active constituents of soybeans, exhibit various biologic char- acteristics possibly associated with anticancer influ- ence, including estrogenic/antiestrogenic19) and antioxidant effects,20) inhibition of TPO-catalyzed reactions6) and ele- vation of UDP-GT.7, 8) In addition, a synthetic plant fla- vonoid (EMD 21388) is known to displace T4 from its binding protein and thus increase serum free T4 in rats.21) The structure (3-methyl-4,6-dihydroxy-3,5-dibromofla- vone) of this compound is somewhat similar to that of soybean genistein (4,5,7-trihydroxyisoflavone). We there- fore thought that feeding the isoflavone might have affected the thyroid hormone level and exerted synergistic goitrogenic effects with ID. However, SI did not affect histopathology or thyroid hormone levels except for the slight T4 decrease in the 0.04% SI alone group with or without ID. These results are in line with a previous study,22) in which isolated soy protein increased T4 concen- trations, but protein from soy protein concentrate, which was water-extracted and thus contained much higher levels of SI, did not. In addition, in our earlier study, isoflavone (400 ppm) and genistein (250 ppm) did not promote thy- roid carcinogenesis due to N-bis(2-hydroxypropyl)nitros- amine in male23) and ovariectomized rats.24) Thus, these results indicate that inhibition of TPO and metabolic excretion by UDP-GT associated with SI may not be the main mechanisms underlying the goitrogenic effects of soybeans, and suggest that isoflavone alone may not be involved in the mechanism underlying the goitrogenic effects of soybean and the synergism with ID. In addition, SI has an estrogenic effect19) and estradiol is known to influence thyroid hormone levels.25, 26) Estrogen acts directly on thyroid tissue via estrogen receptors27) and is known to enhance extra-thyroidal conversion of T4 to T3,25, 26) resulting in increased secretion of TSH and T3, but decreased T4.25) In addition, 17-estadiol promotes N- methyl-N-nitrosourea-induced thyroid carcinogenesis under the influence of ID.28) However, the estrogenic effect of SI is only weak19) and SI did not change T3, T4 and TSH lev- els. Thus, it is possible that the estrogenic effect of SI is also not involved in the thyroid effects of soybean. Although iodine plays a central role in thyroid physiol- ogy, the findings of iodine deficiency studies as a whole remain inconclusive.29) The process of goitrogenesis is likely to be the consequence of an increased TSH stimula- tion linked to an initial reduction of circulating thyroid hormone caused by iodine deficiency.11, 12, 18) In rats main- tained on a low-iodine diet, serum T4 levels decrease to very low values, whereas the T3 level is relatively well maintained.10, 30) The maintenance of serum T3 levels in ID rats involves an increase in thyroidal biosynthesis of T3 relative to T4.30) In the present study, iodine deficiency by itself significantly reduced serum T4 and increased TSH levels, although it rather showed a tendency to increase serum T3 as compared to the control value. However, this might be a spurious finding, since the control value was below the historical control range in our laboratory Table III. title: Ramulus Mori (Sangzhi) alkaloids attenuate diet-induced obesity by modulating adipose tissue metabolic programs
abstract: Obesity is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by excessive adipose accumulation and is closely associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), cardiovascular diseases, and metabolic dysfunction associated steatotic liver disease. Enhancing adipose metabolic activity, including thermogenic- and oxidative-related metabolic programs, has emerged as a promising strategy to counteract obesity and its related metabolic complications. Ramulus Mori (Sangzhi) alkaloids (SZ-A), a natural alkaloid complex derived from Morus alba L. (mulberry twig), are clinically approved for T2DM treatment and exhibit multiple metabolic regulatory properties; however, their anti-obesity mechanisms remain incompletely understood. In this study, male C57BL/6 mice were fed a high-fat diet (HFD) for 14 weeks to induce obesity and subsequently treated with SZ-A (200 or 600 mg/kg) for 6 weeks. SZ-A markedly attenuated HFD-induced body weight gain independent of food intake, improved glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity, and alleviated dyslipidemia and hepatic steatosis. SZ-A also reduced the mass of multiple adipose depots and upregulated thermogenic regulators and beige adipocyte–associated markers in white adipose tissue (WAT). In brown adipose tissue (BAT), SZ-A partially restored HFD-impaired thermogenic features, accompanied by increased expression of uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1). In vitro, SZ-A directly promoted the expression of genes associated with adipocyte browning-related transcriptional features at the transcriptional level in differentiated 3T3-L1 adipocytes, supporting a cell-autonomous metabolic effect. Transcriptomic profiling of epididymal WAT (eWAT) revealed that SZ-A induced broad transcriptional remodeling, characterized by significant enrichment of fatty acid catabolism, β-oxidation, and lipid oxidative metabolism pathways, along with enrichment of brown adipocyte differentiation–related gene signatures. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that SZ-A exerts robust anti-obesity effects through coordinated regulation of adipose tissue metabolism, involving enhanced thermogenic-related molecular features in BAT and inguinal WAT (iWAT) and oxidative metabolic remodeling in visceral adipose tissue. Given its established clinical safety in T2DM, SZ-A represents a promising therapeutic candidate for adipose-based obesity and associated metabolic disorders. Supplementary Information The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-026-45462-9.
full_text: Introduction Obesity has become a major global health concern, with its prevalence continuing to rise across all age groups and regions. Clinical obesity is defined as a chronic, systemic illness characterized by alterations in the function of tissues, organs, the entire individual, or a combination thereof, due to excess adiposity. It can lead to severe end-organ damage, causing life-altering and potentially life-threatening complications such as myocardial infarction, stroke, and renal failure . Based on morphological and functional characteristics, adipose tissue is generally categorized into WAT, BAT, and beige adipose tissue (BeAT) . WAT primarily stores energy in the form of unilocular lipid droplets and mobilizes fatty acids during energy demand. In contrast, BAT is characterized by multilocular lipid droplets and abundant mitochondria, with high expression of UCP1, which mediates non-shivering thermogenesis by dissipating energy as heat to increase whole-body energy expenditure and maintain body temperature . In obese individuals, BAT mass and thermogenic activity are markedly reduced or functionally impaired , while WAT undergoes hypertrophy and excessive lipid accumulation. Notably, the functional boundary between WAT and BAT is not absolute but exhibits plasticity under specific physiological and pharmacological stimuli. Under conditions of chronic cold stress or sustained β3-adrenergic receptor activation , white adipocytes can undergo a phenotypic transition to exhibit brown fat-like characteristics—including multilocular lipid droplet morphology, increased mitochondrial biogenesis, and robust UCP1 expression. This reversible transformation, termed “browning” blurs the classical distinction between energy-storing white fat and energy-dissipating brown fat. The resulting beige adipocytes have been identified in both humans and rodents . Conversely, BAT can acquire white fat-like characteristics such as enlarged lipid droplets, reduced mitochondrial content, and downregulated UCP1 expression, referred to as “whitening”, which commonly occurs in obese individuals and worsens with obesity severity. Given the limited BAT mass in adult humans, strategies aimed at promoting WAT browning and restoring adipose thermogenic potential have attracted considerable interest as potential therapeutic approaches for obesity and related metabolic disorders . SZ-A are a group of natural alkaloid compounds derived from Morus alba L. (mulberry twig), accounting for more than 50% of the total extract. The major bioactive constituents include 1-deoxynojirimycin (1-DNJ), fagomine (FA), and 1,4-dideoxy-1,4-imino-D-arabinitol (DAB). SZ-A has been clinically approved for the treatment of T2DM in China since 2020 . Previous studies have shown that SZ-A is rapidly absorbed and distributed to multiple metabolic target tissues, where it exerts broad metabolic benefits, including improved glucose homeostasis, reduced systemic inflammation, and regulated lipid metabolism . Adipose tissue is the primary target of anti-obesity treatment. Several natural compounds have been reported to induce WAT browning, thereby exerting anti-obesity effects . However, it remains unclear whether SZ-A can regulate adipose thermogenic programs and metabolic remodeling of adipose tissue under obese conditions. In this study, we systematically evaluated the anti-obesity potential of SZ-A and its regulatory effects on adipose tissue metabolism in vivo and in vitro. Using a high-fat diet–induced obese mouse model and differentiated adipocyte cultures, we investigated the impact of SZ-A on adipose tissue morphology, metabolic parameters, and thermogenic markers. Our results demonstrate that SZ-A ameliorates HFD-induced obesity and metabolic disturbances independent of changes in food intake. This is accompanied by a significant upregulation of UCP1 and peroxisome proliferator–activated receptor gamma coactivator 1α (PGC-1α) protein levels in BAT, alongside the induction of browning-associated molecular signatures in white adipose tissues. Materials and methods Chemicals and reagents SZ-A powder was kindly provided by Beijing Wehand-Bio Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). For experimental use, SZ-A was dissolved in saline for oral gavage in animals or in cell culture medium to prepare designated concentrations for in vitro studies. Animals and treatment Male C57BL/6 mice (7 weeks old, 20–25 g) were purchased from Chengdu Dossy Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Mice were housed under controlled conditions (n = 3 per. Cage, 23 ± 2 °C, 12 h light/dark cycle, 50–60% humidity) with free access to food and water. After one-week acclimatization period, mice were randomly divided into two groups: standard diet (SD; 10% fat, 20% protein, 70% carbohydrate; n = 6) or high-fat diet (HFD; 60% fat, 20% protein, 20% carbohydrate; n = 18) (XTHF60 vs XTCON50J, Xietong Shengwu, Jiangsu, China) to induce obesity. After 14 weeks of feeding, HFD-fed mice were further randomized into three groups (n = 6 per group): HFD control (HFD), low-dose SZ-A group (HFD + SZL; 200 mg/kg/day), and high-dose SZ-A group (HFD + SZH; 600 mg/kg/day). The SD and HFD control groups received equivalent volumes of normal saline by oral gavage, while the SZ-A groups were administered high/low dose of SZ-A daily for 6 weeks. Body weight and food intake were recorded weekly. At the end of the experiment, all animals were anesthetized with isoflurane (RWD Life Science, China) delivered via inhalation. Mice were placed in an induction chamber and exposed to 5% isoflurane in oxygen for approximately 10 s until deep anesthesia was achieved, as confirmed by the absence of righting and pedal withdrawal reflexes. Euthanasia was performed under anesthesia in all animals. Specifically, mice were euthanized by cervical dislocation in accordance with the institutional guidelines and the AVMA Guidelines for the Euthanasia of Animals. Death was confirmed by the complete cessation of heartbeat, which was assessed visually and by gentle palpation of the thoracic area. Following confirmation of death, organs and tissue samples were dissected, weighed, immediately frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored at − 80 °C until further analysis. The study was carried out in compliance with the ARRIVE guidelines ( https://arriveguidelines.org ). All animal procedures were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Approval No. 20240926002). Glucose tolerance test and insulin tolerance test Tolerance tests were performed within 3 days after the completion SZ-A administration. Before intraperitoneal glucose tolerance test (ipGTT) or insulin tolerance test (ipITT), mice were fasted for 12 h. On the day of the experiment, mice were weighed and injected intraperitoneally with glucose (1 g/kg, dissolved in sterile saline) or insulin (0.5 U/kg, dissolved in sterile saline). Blood glucose levels were measured from tail vein samples at 0, 15, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min after injection using glucometers (Roche, Germany). Blood lipid profile analysis Blood samples were collected from mice fasted for 12 h via cardiac puncture. The blood samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C to obtain serum samples which were then stored at −80 °C. Serum levels of total cholesterol (TC), triglycerides (TG), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) were measured using the automatic biochemical analyzer (Servicebio, Wuhan, China) according to standard procedures outlined in the Chinese National Standards. Cell culture and adipogenic differentiation Mouse 3T3-L1 preadipocytes were purchased from Wuhan Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (P/S) at 37 °C in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO₂. For adipogenic differentiation, 3T3-L1 preadipocytes were cultured until full confluence and maintained for 2 days to achieve contact inhibition. Differentiation was then induced by incubation in DMEM containing 10% FBS, 0.5 mM 3-isobutyl-1-methylxanthine (IBMX), 1 μM dexamethasone, 1 μM rosiglitazone, and 2 μg/mL insulin for 3 days. The medium was then replaced with DMEM containing 10% FBS, 1 μM rosiglitazone, and 2 μg/mL insulin for 4 days, followed by maintenance in DMEM with 10% FBS for another 2 days to complete differentiation. Cell viability assay 3T3-L1 preadipocytes were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 4–6 × 10 cells/mL in 100 μL complete medium per well. After incubation for 12 h at 37 °C and 5% CO₂ atmosphere, the medium was replaced with 200 μL of complete medium containing various concentrations of SZ-A (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL). Normal control and blank wells were included, with 6 replicates per group. After 24, 48, and 72 h of incubation, 100 μL of CCK-8 working solution (GlpBio, USA) was added to each well and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. The optical density (OD) was then measured at 450 nm using a multifunctional microplate reader (BioTek, USA), and cell viability was calculated based on OD values relative to the control group. Oil Red O staining The Oil Red O stock solution was prepared by dissolving 300 mg of Oil Red O powder (Sigma, USA) in 100 mL of 100% isopropanol and stored at 4 °C in the dark. The working solution (0.2%) was freshly prepared by mixing the stock solution with ultrapure water at a ratio of 3:2, followed by filtration. After differentiation, 3T3-L1 adipocytes were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. The fixed cells were washed with ultrapure water, incubated with 60% isopropanol for 2 min, and then stained with 0.2% Oil Red O working solution at room temperature for 10 min. Following staining, cells were washed thoroughly with water until the rinse became clear. Stained lipid droplets were observed under an inverted microscope (Olympus BX53M, Japan). For quantitative analysis, the retained dye was eluted with isopropanol, and absorbance was measured at 520 nm using a spectrophotometer. Quantitative PCR analysis Total RNA was extracted using TRIzol reagent (Oriscience, China) and reverse-transcribed into cDNA using a reverse transcription kit (Vazyme, China). Quantitative PCR was performed using SYBR Green Master Mix (Vazyme, China) on a real-time PCR system. β-actin served as the internal control for normalization. Primer sequences are listed in S1 Table. Histological and immunohistochemical analysis BAT and WAT samples were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde, dehydrated, paraffin-embedded, and sectioned. For histological evaluation, tissue sections were deparaffinized and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Immunohistochemistry (IHC) was performed to detect UCP1 expression in BAT and WAT using an anti-UCP1 antibody (Abcam, UK). Hepatic lipid accumulation was assessed by Oil Red O staining. Liver tissues were rinsed with PBS, embedded in OCT compound, and sectioned at a thickness of 8–10 μm. The frozen sections were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, stained with Oil Red O working solution, counterstained with hematoxylin, rinsed, and mounted. Images were acquired using the SLIDEVIEW VS200 scanner (Olympus, Japan) and quantitatively analyzed with ImageJ software. Western blotting Adipose tissues were lysed in RIPA buffer (Beyotime Biotech, China) containing protease and phosphatase inhibitors. Protein concentrations were determined using a BCA Protein Assay Kit (Beyotime Biotech, China). Equal amounts of protein samples were separated by 4–12% SDS–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS–PAGE) and transferred onto polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes (Millipore, USA). The membranes were blocked with 5% BSA for 1 h at room temperature and then incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies, including β-tubulin (1:5000, Affinity Biosciences, China) and UCP1 (1:1000, HuaAn Biotechnology, China). After washing, the membranes were incubated for 1 h at room temperature with secondary antibodies—anti-rabbit IgG (1:5000, Bioswamp, China) or anti-mouse IgG (1:5000, Proteintech, USA). Protein bands were visualized using enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) reagents (Oriscience, China) and analyzed with a Chemi-Doc imaging system (E-Blot, USA). Western blot bands were quantified by densitometry using ImageJ. Briefly, images were acquired under non-saturating conditions and analyzed with identical rectangular selections for each lane. Background subtraction was performed uniformly across all lanes. The intensity of each target protein band was normalized to the corresponding housekeeping protein (β-tubulin) from the same sample. Normalized values were expressed relative to the control group (set to 1.0) and are presented as mean ± SEM from independent biological replicates. Statistical comparisons were performed as described in the Statistical Analysis section. RNA sequencing and data analysis Tanscriptomic sequencing of eWAT was performed by Novogene Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Total RNA was extracted from eWAT using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen, USA). RNA concentration and integrity were examined with the Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer system (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) to ensure that the samples met the quality requirements for library construction. Poly(A)-tailed mRNA was enriched from total RNA using oligo(dT)-attached magnetic beads, followed by fragmentation and cDNA synthesis. The obtained cDNA fragments were purified using the AMPure XP system (Beckman Coulter, Beverly, MA, USA) and amplified by PCR to generate the final sequencing libraries. Library quality was assessed using the Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer and quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR). High-quality libraries were sequenced on the Illumina NovaSeq platform to generate 150 bp paired-end reads. RNA sequencing data were uploaded to the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) under accession number ( GSE312093 ). Clean reads were aligned to the Ensembl mouse reference genome (mm10/GRCm38) using HISAT2 software (version 2.0.5). Differential expression analysis was conducted using the DESeq2 R package (version 1.20.0), and p-values were adjusted by the Benjamini–Hochberg method to control the false discovery rate (FDR). Genes with an FDR-adjusted p-value (padj) ≤ 0.05 and |log₂(fold change)|≥ 1 were defined as differentially expressed genes (DEGs). Furthermore, gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was performed based on the identified DEGs to evaluate the transcriptional regulation of lipid metabolism and adipose tissue browning pathways following SZ-A treatment. The GSEA analysis was carried out by Novogene (Beijing, China). Statistical analysis All data are expressed as mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM). Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 10. Comparisons among multiple groups were conducted using one-way analysis of variance (one-way ANOVA). When analyses involved two independent variables, two-way analysis of variance (two-way ANOVA) was applied, followed by appropriate post hoc multiple-comparison tests when necessary. A value of p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Results SZ-A reduces body weight gain and adipose accumulation induced by HFD During the 20-week feeding experiment, mice in the HFD group exhibited a significantly higher body weight compared with those fed a standard diet (SD) ( p < 0.0001) (Fig. A). Following 6 weeks of SZ-A administration starting from week 14, body weight gain in HFD-fed mice was markedly suppressed in a dose-dependent manner, with the high-dose SZ-A group (600 mg/kg) showing the more pronounced effect ( p < 0.0001) (Fig. A–B). Importantly, SZ-A treatment did not significantly affect the average weekly food intake ( p = 0.0573) (Fig. C). In summary, the consistent improvements in adiposity-related endpoints without changes in caloric intake support a direct adipose-targeted effect of SZ-A. ( A ) Body weight changes over 20 weeks in mice fed SD, HFD, HFD + SZ-A (200 mg/kg), and HFD + SZ-A (600 mg/kg); ( B ) Total body weight gain; ( C ) Average weekly food intake; ( D – F ) Ratios of eWAT, iWAT, and BAT to body weight; ( G – H ) Liver and kidney weights; ( I ) Representative images of Oil Red O staining in liver sections (scale bar = 100 μm); ( J ) Quantitative analysis of hepatic lipid deposition area. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM (n = 5–6). Significance levels: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, **** p < 0.0001 compared with HFD; ns not significant. To determine whether the attenuation of body weight gain was associated with reduced tissue mass accumulation, the weights of major adipose depots and metabolic organs were measured. Compared with the SD group, HFD feeding markedly increased the fat-to-body weight ratios of eWAT, iWAT, and BAT, as well as liver and kidney weights (all p < 0.0001) (Fig. D–F). In contrast, SZ-A administration significantly reduced tissue mass in a dose-dependent manner. Relative to HFD-fed mice, SZ-A treatment reduced iWAT mass by approximately 26.7% and 37.9%, eWAT mass by 14.4% and 18.1%, and BAT mass by 42.5% and 49.8% at doses of 200 mg/kg and 600 mg/kg, respectively ( p < 0.05) (Fig. D–F). In parallel, liver weight was reduced by approximately 33.4% and 40.6%, while kidney weight decreased by 15.9% and 21.0% at the corresponding doses ( p < 0.05) (Fig. G–H). Previous studies have demonstrated that HFD commonly leads to hepatic lipid accumulation and steatosis . Consistent with these findings, Oil Red O staining revealed a marked increase in both the number and size of lipid droplets in hepatocytes of HFD-fed mice ( p < 0.0001). In contrast, SZ-A treatment notably alleviated hepatic lipid accumulation in a dose-dependent manner, with the high-dose group showing the most prominent improvement ( p < 0.0001) (Fig. I–J). These findings indicate that SZ-A effectively protects against HFD-induced hepatic steatosis and excessive fat deposition. SZ-A improves glucose and lipid metabolism disorders in HFD-fed mice To further evaluate the metabolic effects of SZ-A, parameters related to glucose and lipid homeostasis were assessed. The ipGTT demonstrated that HFD-fed mice exhibited significantly higher blood glucose levels after glucose challenge and an increased area under the curve (AUC) compared with SD group ( p < 0.0001), indicating impaired glucose tolerance. In contrast, SZ-A administration reduced the blood glucose peak, accelerated glucose clearance, and significantly decreased the AUC ( p < 0.05) (Fig. A–B), suggesting that SZ-A effectively ameliorated HFD-induced glucose intolerance. Similarly, results from the ipITT revealed that HFD-fed mice displayed blunted responses to insulin stimulation, as evidenced by a smaller glucose reduction and a significantly higher AUC ( p < 0.0001), indicating insulin resistance. High-dose SZ-A treatment led to a greater decline in blood glucose and a significantly reduced AUC ( p < 0.01) (Fig. C–D), demonstrating enhanced insulin sensitivity in obese mice. Consistent with the reduction in liver mass and alleviation of fat accumulation, high-dose SZ-A treatment significantly lowered fasting blood glucose levels ( p < 0.05) (Fig. E). ( A ) ipGTT blood glucose curves; ( B ) AUC of ipGTT; ( C ) ipITT blood glucose curves; ( D ) AUC of ipITT; ( E ) Fasting blood glucose levels; ( F ) Serum HDL levels; ( G ) Serum LDL levels; ( H ) Serum TC levels; ( I ) Serum TG levels. Data are presented as mean ± SEM (n = 5–6). Significance levels: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, **** p < 0.0001 compared with HFD; ns not significant. In terms of lipid metabolism, serum levels of TC, TG, LDL-C, and HDL-C were all significantly elevated in HFD-fed mice relative to SD group ( p < 0.05), indicating pronounced dyslipidemia (Fig. F–H). High-dose SZ-A administration significantly reduced these elevated lipid parameters ( p < 0.01) (Fig. F–H), suggesting a corrective effect on HFD-induced lipid abnormalities. Collectively, these results demonstrate that SZ-A exerts systemic regulatory effects on glucose and lipid metabolism in HFD-fed mice by improving insulin resistance and enhancing insulin sensitivity, thereby highlighting its therapeutic potential in the prevention and treatment of obesity and related metabolic syndromes. SZ-A differentially remodels adipocyte morphology and thermogenic-related gene programs in iWAT and eWAT To further investigate the impact of SZ-A on adipose tissue, histological and molecular biological analyses were performed on iWAT and eWAT. H&E staining revealed that the HFD group exhibited markedly enlarged lipid droplets and a significant increase in average adipocyte area compared to the SD group ( p < 0.0001). In contrast, SZ-A treatment significantly attenuated adipocyte hypertrophy in both iWAT and eWAT, with the high-dose group demonstrating a more pronounced therapeutic effect ( p < 0.0001) (Fig. A–B). These findings indicate that SZ-A markedly attenuates HFD-induced adipocyte hypertrophy. ( A ) H&E staining and UCP1 IHC staining of iWAT and eWAT (scale bar = 200 μm); ( B ) Quantitative analysis of average adipocyte area (μm ) in iWAT and eWAT; ( C ) Quantitative analysis of UCP1 protein expression in iWAT and eWAT assessed by AOD from IHC staining; ( D ) Relative mRNA expression levels of thermogenic and browning-related genes (Cidea, Tmem26, Cd137, Ucp1, Pgc-1α, Pparγ, Prdm16) in iWAT; ( E ) Relative mRNA expression levels of thermogenic and browning-related genes in eWAT. Data are presented as mean ± SEM (n = 5–6). Significance levels: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, **** p < 0.0001 compared with HFD; ns, not significant. Regarding the expression of thermogenic proteins, IHC staining showed that the average optical density (AOD) of UCP1 in both iWAT and eWAT was significantly lower in the HFD group compared to the SD group ( p < 0.0001) (Fig. A, C). Following SZ-A administration, UCP1 protein expression in iWAT recovered in a dose-dependent manner, with the high-dose group showing significantly higher AOD values than the HFD group ( p < 0.001). While an upward trend in UCP1 AOD was also observed in eWAT, the magnitude of induction was relatively modest, suggesting a tissue-specific regulatory pattern of UCP1 expression by SZ-A across different fat depots (Fig. C). This depot-specific difference is consistent with the distinct thermogenic capacities of subcutaneous and visceral fat depots. At the gene expression level, SZ-A treatment—particularly at high doses—significantly upregulated the expression of multiple thermogenic and beige adipocyte markers in iWAT, including Ucp1, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (Pparγ), Pgc-1α, PR domain containing 16 (Prdm16), cluster of differentiation 137 (Cd137), and cell death-inducing DFFA-like effector A (Cidea) (all p < 0.05) (Fig. D). The restoration of Ucp1, Pgc-1α, and Prdm16 was most prominent, suggesting that SZ-A activates a browning-related transcriptional program in iWAT. In contrast, changes in Tmem26 expression in iWAT remained limited. In eWAT, SZ-A treatment similarly induced significant gene expression remodeling. Compared with the HFD group, high-dose SZ-A significantly upregulated the expression of Pparγ, Prdm16, and Cidea ( p < 0.05) (Fig. E). However, gene expression levels of Ucp1 and Pgc-1α exhibited only a mild recovery trend and did not return to SD levels. Furthermore, no significant changes were observed for Tmem26 and Cd137 in eWAT. These results suggest that in visceral adipose tissue, SZ-A primarily induces metabolic transcriptional remodeling rather than a classical, robust UCP1-dependent browning. In summary, SZ-A treatment significantly suppresses HFD-induced adipocyte hypertrophy and induces a distinct browning phenotype in iWAT, while promoting molecular alterations characterized by metabolic remodeling in eWAT. This tissue-specific regulatory mode suggests that SZ-A remodels adipose tissue molecular characteristics in obese mice by differentially modulating the metabolic programs of distinct fat depots. SZ-A restores BAT thermogenic program in HFD-fed mice Under obese conditions, BAT can undergo a process known as “whitening,” which is characterized by excessive lipid droplet accumulation, disrupted tissue architecture, and suppression of thermogenic-related molecular programs . To determine whether SZ-A modulates BAT remodeling under high-fat diet (HFD) conditions, histological and molecular analyses were performed on BAT samples from experimental mice. H&E staining revealed that BAT from HFD-fed mice exhibited pronounced lipid droplet enlargement, reduced cellular density, and disorganized tissue structure, consistent with a whitening phenotype. In contrast, SZ-A administration markedly attenuated lipid accumulation and improved BAT morphology, with the high-dose group showing the most prominent restoration toward a compact, multilocular appearance (Fig. A). Notably, the reduction in BAT mass observed following SZ-A treatment was accompanied by improved tissue architecture, suggesting that this change reflects decreased lipid deposition rather than atrophy of thermogenic tissue. IHC analysis further demonstrated that UCP1 expression, quantified as AOD, was significantly reduced in the HFD group compared with the SD group. SZ-A treatment partially restored UCP1 protein expression in BAT, as evidenced by a significant increase in UCP1 AOD, particularly in the high-dose group ( p < 0.0001) (Fig. A–B). Consistent with these histological observations, qPCR analysis showed that SZ-A treatment significantly upregulated the expression of multiple genes associated with brown adipocyte identity and thermogenic regulation in BAT, including Ucp1, Pgc-1α, Pparγ, Prdm16, Cidea, and Cd137, compared with the HFD group. The magnitude of induction was most pronounced in mice receiving high-dose SZ-A (all p < 0.05) (Fig. C). To further validate these molecular changes at the protein level, Western blot analysis was performed. Compared with the HFD group, BAT from the high-dose SZ-A–treated mice exhibited significantly elevated protein levels of UCP1 ( p < 0.001) and PGC-1α ( p < 0.01) (Fig. D–E). Given the established role of PGC-1α as a central regulator of mitochondrial biogenesis and oxidative metabolism, the coordinated upregulation of PGC-1α and UCP1 suggests that SZ-A activates a thermogenic-related gene program and improves mitochondrial regulatory capacity in BAT. Collectively, these results demonstrate that SZ-A alleviates HFD-induced BAT whitening by reducing lipid droplet accumulation and restoring the expression of thermogenic- and mitochondrial-related molecular markers. Although direct functional measurements of heat production or whole-body energy expenditure were not performed, the observed structural and molecular changes are consistent with an enhanced thermogenic potential and improved metabolic properties of BAT under high-fat diet conditions. SZ-A induces browning-associated transcriptional signatures in 3T3-L1 adipocytes in vitro To further determine whether SZ-A exerts similar browning effects in vitro, 3T3-L1 preadipocytes were used as a cellular model. The potential cytotoxicity of SZ-A was first evaluated using the CCK-8 assay after 72-h exposure to increasing concentrations (6.25–400 μg/mL). As shown in Fig. A, SZ-A exhibited no cytotoxicity up to 50 μg/mL, whereas cell viability significantly decreased at 100 μg/mL and above ( p < 0.05). Therefore, concentrations of 50 and 100 μg/mL were selected for subsequent experiments. Oil Red O staining revealed that SZ-A treatment was associated with moderate alterations in lipid droplet morphology in mature adipocytes (Fig. B). Consistently, quantitative analysis showed a modest reduction in Oil Red O staining intensity in SZ-A–treated cells compared with the control group ( p < 0.05) (Fig. C). These changes were modest and were not the primary focus of the in vitro analysis. At the gene expression level, qPCR analysis revealed that treatment with 100 μg/mL SZ-A significantly upregulated the expression of beige adipocyte marker genes—Cidea and cytochrome c oxidase subunit 8B (Cox8b)—as well as thermogenic regulators Ucp1, Pgc-1α, and Pparγ ( p < 0.05) (Fig. D), in the absence of exogenous adrenergic stimulation. The expression of Prdm16 showed an increasing trend but did not reach statistical significance. Collectively, these results indicate that SZ-A modulates adipocyte metabolic–related molecular features and induces a browning-associated transcriptional signature in 3T3-L1 adipocytes in vitro. Notably, these findings are primarily based on transcriptional and morphological observations and do not directly reflect functional metabolic output; nevertheless, they support a cell-autonomous regulatory effect of SZ-A on adipocyte metabolic gene programs. Transcriptomic profiling and pathway enrichment analysis of eWAT To further elucidate the metabolic regulatory mechanisms of SZ-A, RNA sequencing was performed on eWAT from HFD and high-dose SZ-A–treated HFD mice (n = 5 per group). Principal component analysis (PCA) revealed a clear separation between the two groups (Fig. A), indicating substantial transcriptional reprogramming associated with SZ-A treatment. Hierarchical clustering analysis further confirmed pronounced transcriptomic divergence (Fig. B), while Venn diagram analysis identified 669 genes uniquely expressed in the HFD + SZ-A group (Fig. C). Volcano plot analysis identified 1,604 upregulated and 1,703 downregulated genes (p ≤ 0.05, |log₂FC|≥ 1) (Fig. D). Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) demonstrated significant enrichment of multiple lipid metabolism–related pathways, including fatty acid catabolism, fatty acid oxidation, and lipid oxidation (NES ≈ 1.8–2.0, FDR < 0.2), indicating that SZ-A is associated with transcriptional remodeling toward enhanced oxidative metabolic programs in visceral adipose tissue (Fig. E). Notably, these enrichments reflect coordinated changes at the gene expression level and do not directly imply functional increases in mitochondrial oxidative activity. In addition, a positive enrichment trend was observed for the brown fat cell differentiation pathway (NES = 1.61, FDR = 0.21). Given that eWAT is generally resistant to functional browning, this finding is interpreted as engagement of brown adipocyte–associated gene expression signatures at the transcriptional level, rather than evidence of robust UCP1-dependent thermogenesis. Collectively, these transcriptomic data indicate that SZ-A induces broad transcriptional remodeling in eWAT, particularly in pathways related to lipid metabolism and mitochondrial-associated gene programs. These molecular changes are in line with the observed improvements in adipose tissue morphology and systemic metabolic parameters, while acknowledging that causal links to functional metabolic outcomes require further investigation. Discussion The American Association of Clinical Endocrinology (AACE) redefined obesity in 2017 as an adiposity-based chronic disease (ABCD), recognizing it as a chronic, heterogeneous neurohormonal and metabolic disorder. Its hallmark feature is excess or abnormally distributed adipose tissue that disrupts metabolic balance and impairs organ function. The development of obesity arises from complex interactions among genetic, environmental, and neuroendocrine factors, which collectively disturb energy homeostasis and drive pathological fat accumulation . In recent years, enhancing the thermogenic capacity of adipose tissue has emerged as a promising therapeutic strategy. Activation of BAT and browning of WAT are both associated with UCP1-mediated non-shivering thermogenesis, which contributes to energy dissipation and counteracts lipid accumulation . However, given that obese individuals typically exhibit markedly reduced BAT activity , identifying effective strategies to restore adipose metabolic function under obese conditions remains an important research objective. In the present study, we systematically evaluated the metabolic regulatory effects of SZ-A using a mouse model of HFD-induced obesity. Our findings demonstrate that SZ-A administration mitigates body weight gain and adipose accumulation in a dose-dependent manner, while concurrently improving lipid profiles, glucose tolerance, and insulin sensitivity. Notably, these metabolic improvements occurred in the absence of significant alterations in energy intake, suggesting that the anti-obesity effects of SZ-A are not driven by suppressed food consumption but are more likely associated with a global enhancement of metabolic function in adipose tissue. Histological, immunohistochemical, and molecular analyses further revealed that SZ-A exerts tissue-specific regulatory effects across distinct adipose depots, consistent with prior observations . Within BAT, SZ-A alleviated the HFD-induced“whitening” phenotype and restored cellular morphology, an effect accompanied by the recovery of UCP1 expression. Importantly, the observed reduction in BAT mass following SZ-A treatment is more likely attributable to diminished lipid droplet accumulation rather than atrophy of thermogenic tissue, a finding congruent with the improvements in BAT morphology and the enhancement of UCP1 levels. In the iWAT, which is highly susceptible to browning, SZ-A significantly attenuated adipocyte hypertrophy and upregulated several genes associated with thermogenesis and beige adipocyte identity, including Ucp1, Pgc-1α, and Prdm16. Concurrently, we observed that while SZ-A significantly induced UCP1 expression compared to the HFD group, UCP1 levels did not fully revert to those observed in the SD group. This finding suggests that the metabolic benefits conferred by SZ-A may not rely exclusively on the maximal induction of UCP1. Instead, SZ-A likely exerts its effects through broader regulatory mechanisms—such as the enhancement of insulin sensitivity and metabolic flexibility—thereby contributing to body weight maintenance and metabolic improvement even in the absence of full restoration of thermogenic markers. Regarding visceral adipose tissue, transcriptomic profiling of eWAT revealed a substantial remodeling of the gene expression landscape following SZ-A treatment. Although eWAT is typically not a primary site for functional browning, Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) demonstrated a significant enrichment of pathways related to fatty acid degradation, β-oxidation, and lipid oxidative metabolism. These findings suggest that, within visceral fat depots, SZ-A primarily induces a transcriptional shift toward an oxidative metabolic phenotype rather than classical thermogenesis. By bolstering mitochondrial oxidative pathways, SZ-A may mitigate the lipotoxicity and chronic inflammation typically associated with visceral adipose expansion, thereby providing a mechanistic basis for its systemic insulin-sensitizing effects. Mechanistically, SZ-A may modulate the expression programs of thermogenic genes through the synergistic regulation of lipogenic and thermogenesis-related pathways. At the transcriptional level, SZ-A significantly upregulated several thermogenic and beige adipocyte markers (including Ucp1, Cidea, and Tmem26), as well as key transcriptional regulators Pgc-1α and Prdm16 in both iWAT and BAT. Consistently, WB analysis confirmed that SZ-A elevated PGC-1α protein levels in BAT. PGC-1α is a well-established regulator of mitochondrial biogenesis and operates in coordination with PRDM16 to co-regulate the transcription of brown and beige adipocyte-specific genes . Collectively, these findings support a role for SZ-A in enhancing mitochondrial biogenesis and augmenting the metabolic potential of adipose tissue. Consistent with our findings, previous studies have reported a broad spectrum of metabolic benefits associated with SZ-A, including the preservation of pancreatic β-cells function, amelioration of insulin resistance and chronic inflammation, modulation of gut microbiota composition, enhancement of glucagon-like peptide-1 secretion, and attenuation of hepatic lipid accumulation, alongside potential cardiovascular and renal protective effects . These collective observations further underscore that the metabolic impact of SZ-A is multifactorial and not exclusively restricted to adipose tissue thermogenesis. The regulatory effects of SZ-A on adipocyte molecular characteristics were further supported by in vitro experiments. In differentiated 3T3-L1 adipocytes, SZ-A increased the expression of multiple genes associated with thermogenic regulation and mitochondrial function in a cell-autonomous manner, independent of systemic endocrine influences. These findings suggest that SZ-A possesses the intrinsic capacity to directly reprogram adipocyte metabolic gene programs. It should be emphasized, however, that these in vitro observations primarily reflect transcriptional-level regulation and do not directly indicate functional thermogenic output or changes in energy expenditure. Nevertheless, when integrated with the pronounced metabolic improvements observed in vivo, together with the consistent protein-level upregulation of key thermogenic executors such as UCP1 and PGC-1α in BAT and iWAT, these in vitro results provide important mechanistic support for the activation of thermogenic-related gene programs by SZ-A. Future studies incorporating direct measurements of lipolytic activity and mitochondrial respiration will be required to further define the functional metabolic consequences at the cellular level. Despite the systematic characterization of the anti-obesity potential of SZ-A, several limitations should be acknowledged. First, this study was conducted exclusively in male mice; given the well-recognized sexual dimorphism in adipose tissue metabolism and thermogenic capacity, future studies incorporating female cohorts are warranted. Second, although the 14-week HFD model effectively mimics adult-onset obesity, metabolic responses may differ in juvenile or aged animals. In addition, while SZ-A was administered orally to better reflect its clinical use, the potential involvement of gut microbiota–derived metabolites and its effects on intestinal lipid absorption (e.g., fecal lipid content) remain to be investigated. Importantly, although the reduction in body weight gain occurred in the absence of significant changes in energy intake—a finding consistent with a potential increase in energy dissipation or thermogenic output—direct measurements of whole-body energy expenditure were not performed in this study. As a result, the contribution of enhanced thermogenesis to the observed weight-loss phenotype cannot be conclusively established. Future investigations employing comprehensive metabolic cage analyses (indirect calorimetry), including measurements of oxygen consumption, carbon dioxide production, respiratory exchange ratio, physical activity, and heat production, ideally combined with pair-feeding or body weight–adjusted feeding protocols, will be essential to quantitatively assess energy balance and to further delineate the role of thermogenic mechanisms in SZ-A–mediated anti-obesity effects. Finally, although we observed consistent upregulation of thermogenic-related molecular markers, the absence of gold-standard metabolic assessments—such as hyperinsulinemic–euglycemic clamp studies for definitive insulin sensitivity quantification—limits a more precise dissection of the systemic metabolic contributions of SZ-A. Future studies integrating these physiological assessments with functional mitochondrial analyses will be crucial to fully elucidate the pharmacological profile of SZ-A. In addition, glucose homeostasis and insulin sensitivity in this study were assessed using ipGTT and ipITT. Compared with oral glucose tolerance tests (OGTT), these approaches minimize confounding effects from gastrointestinal absorption kinetics and incretin-mediated responses, thereby enabling a more direct evaluation of systemic glucose clearance and peripheral insulin action. Under this experimental design, our results clearly demonstrate a marked improvement in peripheral insulin sensitivity following SZ-A treatment. Nevertheless, given that SZ-A was administered orally, its metabolic benefits may, at least in part, be mediated through gut-dependent mechanisms, including incretin signaling or gut–adipose axis interactions. Future investigations combining OGTT with relevant hormonal analyses will be necessary to more comprehensively elucidate the role of intestinal pathways in SZ-A–mediated glucose homeostasis. Compared with other natural compounds, SZ-A possesses a clear translational advantage. It has been clinically approved for the treatment of T2DM and is supported by a well-established safety and pharmacological profile. Given the substantial overlap in the pathophysiological basis of T2DM and obesity, SZ-A holds promise as a multi-target therapeutic candidate that may improve adipose tissue metabolic status and restore systemic energy homeostasis, thereby offering a potential integrated strategy for the management of adiposity-based obesity and related metabolic syndromes. Conclusion In conclusion, this study systematically demonstrates that SZ-A counteracts HFD-induced obesity and metabolic disturbances through a coordinated mechanism involving attenuation of lipid accumulation and molecular activation of thermogenic-related programs. By promoting iWAT browning, alleviating BAT whitening, and remodeling eWAT metabolism, SZ-A effectively improves systemic energy homeostasis and insulin sensitivity. Considering its established clinical use, SZ-A holds strong translational potential as a safe therapeutic agent for the integrated management of adipose-based obesity and metabolic syndrome. title: Immunosuppressive effects of (2R,5R)-6-heptyne-2,5-diamine, an inhibitor of polyamine synthesis: II. Beneficial effects on the development of a lupus-like disease in MRL-lpr/lpr mice.
abstract: (2R,5R)-6-heptyne-2,5-diamine (MAP; MDL 72175), a potent irreversible inhibitor of L-ornithine decarboxylase (ODC), possesses immunosuppressive activities in vitro as the result of inhibition of lymphocyte polyamine biosynthesis. The effects of MAP were now studied in vivo in MRL-lpr/lpr female mice, an animal model for human systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Administration of MAP (0.2% in drinking water; drug intake: 0.25-0.35 g/kg body weight/day) to female mice for 15 weeks, starting 8 weeks after birth, reduced by 47% the number of spleen cells, retarded development of lymphadenopathy and, at that time, markedly prolonged the survival of the mice. At week 23, MAP reduced plasma IgG concentrations by 50% whereas, in contrast, those of IgM were elevated 1.5-fold. No statistically significant effects of MAP were observed on plasma levels of anti-DNA autoantibodies although serum anti-RNP and anti-Sm titres tended downwards during treatment. Neither glomerular lesions nor proteinuria were improved by MAP administration. Finally chronic administration of MAP for 45 weeks prolonged the median survival time from 29.75 to 35.5 weeks.

title: THE DEGREE OF COMPENSATORY RENAL HYPERTROPHY FOLLOWING UNILATERAL NEPHRECTOMY
abstract: Compensatory hypertrophy of the kidney in albino rats is increased by an increase in the protein intake. The effect is greater in old rats than young rats. Successive increases in the protein intake are followed by a reduction in the increase in the degree of compensatory renal hypertrophy.

This investigation was designed to determine the influence of similar variations in the protein intake upon the degree of compensatory renal hypertrophy following unilateral nephrectomy. Since the appearance of our first preliminary note on this subject (4) there have been two papers (9, 10) dealing with the influence of the protein intake upon compensatory renal hypertrophy in which the conclusions are quantitatively very much at variance with the data presented here.
